The Senate reconciliation bill, known as the One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA), extends the 2017 TCJA individual tax cuts using a 'current policy' baseline. This bill was passed by the Senate and signed by President Trump on July 4, 2025. The bill's provisions include extending and expanding major components of the TCJA.

The One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA) was signed into law by President Trump on July 4, 2025. This law amends several Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) tax credits, including the Production Tax Credits (45Y). The OBBBA revises domestic-content percentages for sections 45Y and 48E, specifying mandatory percentages (40%–55%) based on when construction begins, including dates in 2025. This indicates that domestic-content requirements are now a condition for receiving the 45Y production tax credit.

The One Big Beautiful Bill Act, signed into law by President Trump on July 4, 2025, modifies several Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) tax credits, including those related to domestic content requirements for Investment Tax Credits (ITC) under section 48E. The Act specifies domestic content percentages for qualified facilities and energy storage technology, making these requirements mandatory for obtaining the ITC. Projects beginning construction after June 16, 2025, must meet specific thresholds, such as 45% for projects starting construction after June 16, 2025, and before January 1, 2026.

The House of Representatives passed a reconciliation bill (H.R. 1) on May 22, 2025, which amends several Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) tax credits, including the Advanced Manufacturing Production Credit (Section 45X). The bill introduces Foreign Entity of Concern (FEOC) restrictions that would require domestic materials or subcomponents for certain clean energy components. Specifically, the bill disallows credits for taxpayers identified as specified foreign entities or foreign-influenced entities and for projects receiving material assistance from prohibited foreign entities after December 31, 2025. This effectively introduces a requirement for domestically-produced materials or subcomponents for the Advanced Manufacturing Production Tax Credits (45X) starting in 2025.

Specifically, it covers tax year 2026, confirming that the reconciliation bill includes a deduction for tip income for that year.

The 2025 reconciliation bill, known as the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, includes a provision for an above-the-line deduction for overtime pay. This deduction is capped at $12,500 for individuals and $25,000 for joint filers, and it applies to tax years 2025-2028, which includes tax year 2026. President Trump signed the legislation on July 4, 2025.

The Senate-passed reconciliation bill, as described in the Penn Wharton Budget Model analysis, does not include any provision that reduces the taxation of Social Security benefits. The bill focuses on extending and expanding major components of the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act, with no mention of altering the taxability of Social Security income.

The 2025 reconciliation bill, known as the 'One Big Beautiful Bill Act,' includes a provision that allows for the deduction of interest paid on vehicle loans. Specifically, it excludes qualified passenger vehicle loan interest from the definition of personal interest in Section 163(h) for the years 2025 through 2028. This deduction is capped at $10,000 per year and phases out for taxpayers with modified adjusted gross income (MAGI) in excess of $100,000 ($200,000 for married taxpayers filing jointly). Therefore, car loan interest will be deductible for tax year 2026.

The 2025 reconciliation bill includes provisions that establish new work requirements for Medicaid recipients. Specifically, non-disabled adult Medicaid recipients must demonstrate at least 80 hours per month of 'community engagement,' such as paid employment, education programs, community volunteering, or other activities, to retain their benefits. This provision is expected to remove up to 1.2 million enrollees from Medicaid coverage. The bill has been passed by the House and will likely have significant impacts on Medicaid recipients.
